labels.title: Phuang Malai
labels.subtitle: Nathan Beard
labels.medium: silicone, paint, foam and jewellery belonging to the artist's late mother

text.content:
silicone, paint, foam and jewellery belonging to the artist's late mother

Commissioned 2025

© Nathan Beard

Perth-born, Melbourne-based artist Nathan Beard draws on his Thai heritage to explore the porousness of culture and memory. The sculpture Phuang Malai is an expanded form of portraiture that pays homage to the artist’s late mother, Noi. This newly commissioned work features fragmented limbs cast from the artist’s own body, entangled with one of his mother’s most precious possessions: her wedding band. This impossible configuration recalls the intangible bond between parent and child, as well as the surreal, disembodied mannequins used in visual merchandising. Through these discordant yet entwined associations, Beard questions the fluidity of value and worth attached to personal objects. Dotted with fake orchids, the national flower of Thailand, _Phuang Malai_ further embeds this oscillation between authenticity, value, memory and identity.
inkjet photographic print on paper\n\nCourtesy of Sione Monū

Based between Canberra and Tāmaki Makaurau/Auckland, Sione Monū is a member of the Tongan diaspora. Much of their work is inspired by _nimamea’a tuikakala_ or the Tongan fine art of flower designing, using _kahoa_ or floral garlands. An art form traditionally practised by women, Monū began making their own using sticky contact paper and florals from the environment of each person they were making them for, resulting in their early series _Canberra Family Portraits_. These images were taken on the artist’s smart phone – a tool they often employ to document their life and as an extension their artistic practice, thus blurring the boundaries between the personal and the public.

An art form traditionally practised by women, Monū began making their own using sticky contact paper and florals from the environment of each person they were making them for, resulting in their early series _Canberra Family Portraits_. These images were taken on the artist’s smart phone – a tool they often employ to document their life and as an extension their artistic practice, thus blurring the boundaries between the personal and the public.
synthetic flowers on foam

These suspended works by Sione Monū reveal the progression of the artist’s interest in _nimamea’a tuikakala_ (Tongan flower design). Their early experiments in garland making, evidenced in the 2015 series _Canberra Family Portraits_ on the adjacent wall, have morphed into the _Ao’ Kakala_ masks worn by the artist as an expression of their Tongan diasporic experience and _leitī_ (feminine gender identity). In _Masked Tongan_, the mask is freed and suspended, cloud like, from the ceiling alongside the star form _Fetu’u_. _Masked Tongan_ is inspired by a red feather headdress worn by Fatafei Poulaho, the Tu’i Tonga or King of the island of Tonga, and documented in a 1777 drawing by John Webber. Here, Monū personalises the form of the headdress. It is vibrant, abundant and sparkling, an expression of a world in which _leitī_ live freely.
synthetic flowers on foam\n\nCollecion of the artist

In _Masked Tongan_, the mask is freed and suspended, cloud like, from the ceiling alongside the star form _Fetu’u_. _Masked Tongan_ is inspired by a red feather headdress worn by Fatafei Poulaho, the Tu’i Tonga or King of the island of Tonga, and documented in a 1777 drawing by John Webber. Here, Monū personalises the form of the headdress. It is vibrant, abundant and sparkling, an expression of a world in which _leitī_ live freely.

type C photograph on paper

Gift of the artist 2017. Donated through the Australian Government's Cultural Gifts Program.

© Ingvar Kenne

Ingvar Kenne’s portrait of Australian fashion model Anneliese Seubert (b. 1973) evokes an uncanny synthetic quality enhanced by the bright lighting and cheery yellow chrysanthemums clasped in Seubert’s outstretched hand. This effect lends the sitter a mannequin-like pallor and recalls the numerous glossy fashion spreads she has appeared in over the years. Seubert won the Ford Models Supermodel of the World competition in 1990, aged 17. Signing with the Paris-based agency Karin, she lived in Paris and then New York for the next two decades, walking the runway for Dior, Hermes, Yves Saint Laurent, Gucci and Versace, and appearing in editorials for magazines such as _Vogue_, _Marie Claire_, _Harper’s Bazaar_ and _Elle_. Ingvar Kenne took this photograph during one of Seubert’s regular trips home. In line with Kenne’s deceptively simplistic and intimate style, there was no hairdresser or makeup artist for the shoot. As Seubert recalled: ‘I’m like, “Oh my god.” Worst nightmare. But I mean, the photos were beautiful, and it was quite an interesting shoot because it was full sunlight.’

video.title: Anneliese Seubert
video.transcript:
The previous year to the year I was in the Dolly Covergirl Competition, Kate Fischer had actually won it, and she was at the same school, at Girls Grammar. My friend said, "Well if she can win it, "you can win it too." So we took some photos and sent them in, and then I was a finalist. And my little blurb, I mean it's quite fashionable if you're into STEM, as everyone is these days, but my favourite subject was maths, and I loved skiing. Because I didn't win, I just started working on weekends and in school holidays. And I think I was in year 10 when I was in the competition, so through year 11 and 12 I gradually started working a bit more.

My first jobs were for a magazine called _Follow Me_. And then a couple of _Vogue_ jobs, _Harper's Bazaar_ was around then as well. When I was 17, I had an Australian _Vogue_ cover which was great, and some advertising clients, and off I went. I kind of did a preliminary trip when I was in year 12, I did the _Ford Supermodel Competition_, and I went to L.A. by myself. I won that competition and then went to New York for a couple of weeks, so I'd had a little taste of what it was going to be like, and then I went back to school.

So I finished year 12, and then off to Paris. And I just started going on castings. I thought I had great pictures in my portfolio, but people over in Paris looked at them and like, "hmm," you know, they're kind of a little bit, "daggy Australian," in a way. So I started working, got more pictures and then started doing shows. Then I got very busy traveling and... so many markets around, there's Paris, Germany, Spain, Italy. I was flying from one job to the next. Sometimes you didn't come home in between. But I was kind of just let free. I don't know if I'd do that with my kids now.

Back in the '90s, you would either get a telephone call from your agency in the afternoon before and check what you had to do for the next day, or sometimes you got a fax. You would have to go and visit a client and show them your pictures, maybe try on some clothes, talk to them about what you'd been doing. It's more of a collaboration. The client and the photographer, usually an art director as well. They will all say, "This is what we'd like." They often have reference images. They can tell you a story.

I used to do John Galliano's fashion shows. And he would always make this huge story, and each character, which was essentially each dress or outfit, had a story and a part in the story. So when you were walking around through the scene, the set, you would be imagining that you were this lady on the rooftop who was running away from something. There was always a little story. But I think there was a difference between putting on a Galliano dress, it just made you feel amazing. So just channeling that and thinking about how fabulous you look is really the way it works, I think. 'Cause if you don't believe it, no one else will.

When I was 24, I was based in Paris. I used to come home three times a year, so I was home for Christmas, home for August, and there was usually either I'd meet mum and dad somewhere, or I came home for an extra trip. And I often worked in Sydney while I was home, and the portrait that's hanging, it was funny, because I was so used to turning up at a shoot and having hair and makeup, and they would turn me into a different character. This time, they're like, "Oh no, we just want you." Like, what are you talking about? Where's the hairdresser? "Oh, we just want it to look like you did your own hair." Are you serious? And no makeup? "No, no, look how beautiful you are!" I'm like, "Oh my god." Worst nightmare. My hair is hard to do for a hairdresser at the best of times, so I was a little bit in shock. But I mean, the photos were beautiful, and it was quite an interesting shoot because it was full sunlight and flash, I think. So the lighting is quite harsh. There's definitely a difference between the light in Australia and the light everywhere else. And, yeah, I've got my little bunch of flowers.

Back in the '90s, you would either get a telephone call

47
00:02:10.870 --> 00:02:13.230
from your agency in the afternoon before

48
00:02:13.230 --> 00:02:14.970
and check what you had to do for the next day,

49
00:02:14.970 --> 00:02:16.920
or sometimes you got a fax.

50
00:02:16.920 --> 00:02:18.670
You would have to go and visit a client

51
00:02:18.670 --> 00:02:21.340
and show them your pictures, maybe try on some clothes,

52
00:02:21.340 --> 00:02:23.720
talk to them about what you'd been doing.

53
00:02:23.720 --> 00:02:25.520
It's more of a collaboration.

54
00:02:25.520 --> 00:02:27.590
The client and the photographer,

55
00:02:27.590 --> 00:02:29.600
usually an art director as well.

56
00:02:29.600 --> 00:02:31.380
They will all say, "This is what we'd like."

57
00:02:31.380 --> 00:02:33.359
They often have reference images.

58
00:02:33.359 --> 00:02:34.820
They can tell you a story.

59
00:02:34.820 --> 00:02:37.800
I used to do John Galliano's fashion shows.

60
00:02:37.800 --> 00:02:40.070
And he would always make this huge story,

61
00:02:40.070 --> 00:02:43.430
and each character, which was essentially each dress

62
00:02:43.430 --> 00:02:46.740
or outfit, had a story and a part in the story.

63
00:02:46.740 --> 00:02:50.980
So when you were walking around through the scene, the set,

64
00:02:50.980 --> 00:02:53.840
you would be imagining that you were this lady

65
00:02:53.840 --> 00:02:56.612
on the rooftop who was running away from something.

66
00:02:56.612 --> 00:02:58.730
There was always a little story.

67
00:02:58.730 --> 00:03:00.290
But I think there was a difference

68
00:03:00.290 --> 00:03:03.250
between putting on a Galliano dress,

69
00:03:03.250 --> 00:03:04.915
it just made you feel amazing.

70
00:03:04.915 --> 00:03:07.350
So just channeling that and thinking

71
00:03:07.350 --> 00:03:08.570
about how fabulous you look

72
00:03:08.570 --> 00:03:10.332
is really the way it works, I think.

73
00:03:10.332 --> 00:03:12.415
(laughs)

74
00:03:13.440 --> 00:03:15.890
'Cause if you don't believe it, no one else will.

75
00:03:17.930 --> 00:03:20.930
When I was 24, I was based in Paris.

76
00:03:20.930 --> 00:03:22.360
I used to come home three times a year,

77
00:03:22.360 --> 00:03:24.620
so I was home for Christmas, home for August,

78
00:03:24.620 --> 00:03:26.550
and there was usually either I'd meet mum and dad

79
00:03:26.550 --> 00:03:30.460
somewhere, or I came home for an extra trip.

80
00:03:30.460 --> 00:03:34.380
And I often worked in Sydney while I was home,

81
00:03:34.380 --> 00:03:36.580
and the portrait that's hanging,

82
00:03:36.580 --> 00:03:39.240
it was funny, because I was so used to turning up

83
00:03:39.240 --> 00:03:41.290
at a shoot and having hair and makeup,

84
00:03:41.290 --> 00:03:43.800
and they would turn me into a different character.

85
00:03:43.800 --> 00:03:45.910
This time, they're like, "Oh no, we just want you."

86
00:03:45.910 --> 00:03:47.400
Like, what are you talking about?

87
00:03:47.400 --> 00:03:48.960
Where's the hairdresser?

88
00:03:48.960 --> 00:03:51.570
"Oh, we just want it to look like you did your own hair."

89
00:03:51.570 --> 00:03:53.210
Are you serious?

90
00:03:53.210 --> 00:03:54.650
And no makeup?

91
00:03:54.650 --> 00:03:56.070
"No, no, look how beautiful you are!"

92
00:03:56.070 --> 00:03:58.470
I'm like, "Oh my god."

93
00:03:58.470 --> 00:03:59.640
Worst nightmare.

94
00:03:59.640 --> 00:04:01.380
My hair is hard to do for a hairdresser

95
00:04:01.380 --> 00:04:04.450
at the best of times, so I was a little bit in shock.

96
00:04:04.450 --> 00:04:06.570
But I mean, the photos were beautiful,

97
00:04:06.570 --> 00:04:08.120
and it was quite an interesting shoot

98
00:04:08.120 --> 00:04:10.453
because it was full sunlight and flash, I think.

99
00:04:10.453 --> 00:04:13.150
So the lighting is quite harsh.

100
00:04:13.150 --> 00:04:14.450
There's definitely a difference

101
00:04:14.450 --> 00:04:16.190
between the light in Australia

102
00:04:16.190 --> 00:04:18.080
and the light everywhere else.

103
00:04:18.080 --> 00:04:22.963
And, yeah, I've got my little bunch of flowers. (laughs)

text.content:
type C photograph on paper

Gift of the artist 2017. Donated through the Australian Government's Cultural Gifts Program.

© Ingvar Kenne

Akira Isogawa (b. 1964), fashion designer, was born in Kyoto, Japan, and first came to Australia at the age of 21. After immigrating, he worked as a waiter while studying fashion design at East Sydney TAFE and Sydney’s National Art School. In 1993 he opened the Akira Isogawa boutique in Woollahra, and swiftly established a reputation for his bold and intelligent clothes. At Sydney’s Australian Fashion Week in 1996, he sent all his models out wearing red socks from Gowings because he couldn’t afford shoes for them; but he has shown at every Fashion Week since, and since 1998 he has shown twice annually in Paris. He has exhibited at the Museum of Contemporary Art, and Sydney’s Object Gallery’s exhibition of his construction techniques is said to have been its most successful ever show. The National Gallery of Victoria’s _Akira Isogawa: Printemps Eté_ in 2004-2005 was the first solo exhibition of an Australian designer’s work to be shown at one of its major art institutions and later toured to Singapore, Manila and Bangkok. In 2005 Isogawa was depicted on the ‘Australian Legends’ series of postage stamps and in 2007 he was named the country’s first Fashion Laureate. He has designed costumes for four Sydney Dance Company productions and for the Australian Ballet’s production of Graeme Murphy’s _Romeo and Juliet_ in 2010. Sydney’s Museum of Applied Arts and Sciences showed the retrospective _Akira Isogawa_ at 2018-2019. Since the Woollahra boutique closed in 2017, Australia’s only two Akira boutiques are in the Strand Arcade, Sydney, and Fortitude Valley, Brisbane.

audio.title: Akira Isogawa
audio.transcript: When I look back, being a teenager growing up in a traditional city in Japan called Kyoto, I felt I was a bit of a fashion victim, really. Well now actually I create fashion. But back then, actually, I was consuming fashion and that's how much I loved [it]. \nJapanese culture is so different from Western ones. My grandparents had a house next door. They were all wearing actually traditional clothes – kimono. What I noticed about kimono is actually they're made out rectangular shaped cloth. And they have no shapes. Whereas actually, Western clothes has a particular shape that enhance, particularly actually for female, the femininity of female body, but whereas actually kimono is totally unisex. I mean, I've never actually designed kimono myself. But being a kimono designer, I imagine actually, they work on texture, colour, print, and so on, because it's a highly textile driven fashion, and then to me, that's what Japanese fashion is all about. You know, when I design my own collection, I never really force myself to express my Japanese entity, but I feel that it just comes up naturally. And I think what's interesting is that not just, you know, designing something so traditionally Japanese, but then I kind of actually manage to fuse with Australian ease I guess actually. Australia has got this particular attitude – friendly to start with – but it's quite actually casual about things, you know. I feel actually my design is a result of actually, you know, some Japanese heritage with Australian attitude, which is actually friendly and easy.
audio.acknowledgements: This recording was made during interviews for the National Portrait Gallery's [_Portrait Stories_][1] series.\n\n [1]: /stories

text.content:
inkjet print on paper

Collection: National Portrait Gallery Purchased 2012

© Estate of Robert Rooney

In the late 1970s and early 1980s Robert Rooney took a series of 75 photographic portraits of the Melbourne art scene, often set in suburban houses or streetscapes. Rooney’s subjects included surrealist painter Peter Booth (b. 1940), who gained widespread recognition through the inclusion of his large architectural ‘block’ paintings in the National Gallery of Victoria’s exhibition _The Field_ in 1968. Rooney captured Booth outside his studio with a cigarette; the bouquet of roses next to him innocuous against the concrete and corrugated iron of the inner-city landscape. Of the series Rooney wrote: ‘These portraits were to be as perfect as I could make them. There were certain rules – to use available light, no matter how dim (long exposures and a hand-held camera), and no cropping.’

labels.title: Study for a portrait of Dame Merlyn Myer DBE
labels.subtitle: Sir William Dargie CBE

text.content:
oil on masonite

Gift of the Estate of Marion Orme Page 2016

© Roger Dargie and Faye Dargie

Eight-time Archibald Prize winner Sir William Dargie CBE painted at least two portraits of Melbourne-born philanthropist Dame Merlyn Myer DBE (1900–1982). This portrait depicts the matriarch at ease on a floral-patterned sofa, wearing an elegant, yet relaxed, floor-length black kaftan. To her left is a simple vase filled with overblown pale pink peonies, which signify love, romance, affection and happiness. Following her husband Sidney Myer’s death in 1934, Myer established the Sidney Myer Charitable Trust (later Fund), was a Board member of the Royal Melbourne Hospital for four decades, and was a patron of many organisations. Pinned to Myer’s dress in this portrait is her Badge of the Damehood of the Order of the British Empire, which she was awarded in 1960 for her service to the Red Cross and her tireless philanthropy for cancer and heart disease research.

papercut silhouette drawing

Collection: National Portrait Gallery Purchased 2019

© Pamela See

In Pamela See (Xue Mei-Ling)’s _Making Chinese shadows (sixteen silhouette portraits)_, flowers are used as an allegory for the diasporic experience. See is a Chinese-Australian artist who questions, honours and investigates her cultural inheritance and family experience through her artistic practice. Her work connects the traditions of European silhouette portraiture and Chinese papercutting from Guangdong Province, where her mother’s family originates. In the series of 16 intricate paper cuts from which these works derive, See represents members of the Chinese-Australian community who made major but often unrecognised contributions to society in the 19th century. See says of her motivation for making the portraits: ‘these personal histories became invisible. So it wasn’t just that they were excluded from parts of community life, they just were never recorded. They didn’t exist, more or less.’ Each work incorporates a silhouette based on a photograph, an object representing their achievement, and a floral symbol of an introduced, invasive or endemic species including red silk cotton tree flower (symbolic of Guangdong Province), jasmine flower, common heath and hibiscus.

audio.title: Chang Woo Gow
audio.transcript:
The portrait of Chang Woo Gow made in 2017 by Pamela See is a papercut silhouette drawing on a pale pink background measuring 48.5 centimetres by 38.5 centimetres in its frame. Two black figures face the same direction presenting their right profile. A large white papercut flower floats in the space above them.

The flower lies on its side, a stem holding two large leaves and a full-blown peony. Delicate cuts in the paper allow the pink of the background to come through and indicate the veins on the leaves and the detail of the rose peony.

Directly below the flower and equal to its size is a small, seated woman. Her hair is in a neat bun and her arms are resting on the arms of the chair. Her long robe sweeps the floor and the fine legs of the chair extend out behind her. 

At a slight distance from the seated woman stands a Chinese man, Chang Woo Gow, also in profile. His hair is drawn back in a long braid, and he is dressed in layered long robes. His right hand is stretched out towards his wife’s shoulder, but they do not touch. This creates an interesting negative shape between and around them. As she is seated, she appears to be half his size, emphasizing the radical difference in their height. 

The black silhouettes seem to float in space, and the detail of the white papercut flower creates a well-balanced contrast in this work.
audio.attribution: Audio description script written by Krysia Kitch and voiced by Kate Matthews

inkjet photographic print on paper

Courtesy of the artist and Vivien Anderson Gallery

© Naomi Hobson

Naomi Hobson worked with men from her Lockhart River and Coen communities on the Cape York Peninsula to produce these affectionate intergenerational portraits of Kaantju and Umpila boys, men and Elders. Reflecting on the impact of Christian evangelists and their imposition of restrictive gender roles, Hobson counters negative stereotypes of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander men in the public domain by instead affirming their capacity for caring and beauty. Through the ‘missionisation’ of First Nations peoples, much of the cultural adornment practised by Kaantju and Umpila men ceased. By photographing her male subjects decorated with flowers from the Coen area, Hobson reinvigorates this aspect of her culture and pays tribute to the nurturing compassion of Aboriginal men.

inkjet print on paper

Purchased 2021

© Jesse Lizotte

Photographer Jesse Lizotte wanted to create a portrait of Jimmy Barnes AO (b. 1956) and Jane Barnes (b. 1958) that signifies their enduring love and challenges conventional ‘rock and roll’ depictions of the singer. In this photograph, he captures the tenderness between the spouses as they lie quite literally on a bed of roses. Lizotte, who is also Jimmy and Jane’s nephew, recalls asking them for references for the portrait: ‘Jane had come to me with a French love poem, alongside a series of paintings by Chagall ... I wanted to create a floating illusion, and to incorporate the roses that Jimmy picks from their garden and arranges for Jane every day.’ Not just a romantic backdrop, the couple often give each other rose bushes for their garden as gifts for special occasions. The metaphor of the rose is not lost on them. Considering the flower in relation to their relationship, the couple observed: ‘It’s not always a bed of roses. It’s not always easy, there’s thorns ... but it’s about living through that and sharing those experiences.’

audio.title: Jimmy and Jane Barnes
audio.transcript:
**Jimmy Barnes**: We have a beautiful garden at home and friends would give me roses. We don't want gifts.

**Jane Barnes:** Yeah, for your 60th, what happened was everybody brought a rose. So we created this …

**Jimmy Barnes:** Rose garden

**Jane Barnes:** … amazing rose garden.

**Jimmy Barnes:** Yeah we’ve got these beautiful roses that obviously are brought to us with love and every day I'd go and pick them, roses for Jane all through lockdown. And every day I'd pick a new bunch and say, look at this, isn't this great? And they smell beautiful. And that was sort of my way of reaching out to Jane every day and letting her know I loved her. And it was a simple thing. And I just loved the fact that these roses kept growing in our garden.

**Jane Barnes:** And you could pick a hundred of them.

**Jesse Lizotte:** Well, on the day you brought like wheelbarrows full, you came into the photo studio with just tonnes of roses.

**Jimmy Barnes:** It was beautiful.

**Jesse Lizotte:** Yeah, we had too many. It smelt great.

**Jimmy Barnes:** And that whole metaphor, there’s a portrait about love and the whole metaphor of the roses and things, you know love's not always easy …

**Jane Barnes:** A bed of roses.

**Jimmy Barnes:** … it's not always a bed of rose, it's not always easy …

**Jane Barnes:** Its full of thorns.

**Jimmy Barnes:** ... it takes work. And there's thorns, it's not always going to be great. But it's about living through that and sharing those experiences that makes love. You know, love takes work and love takes effort and love takes... you know, you have to give and take, you know. And I thought it was …

**Jane Barnes:** And adventures and wonderful things as well.

**Jimmy Barnes:** I thought it was a great metaphor with the roses. And particularly because they came from our garden. They were the roses that we've been sharing for the previous years, you know.
