text.content: C-Type photograph on Slickrock Metallic paper\n\nPurchased with lead funds provided by the Dick and Pip Smith Foundation, the Nelson Meers Foundation and the Portrait Dinner Series 2025

Bidjara man Dr Christian Thompson AO has featured native flowering plants in his photographs to explore kinship, culture and connection to Country since the late 2000s. The multi-panel photograph _The Shortest Day_ depicts a large-scale flower-wall, or floral constellation, within which the artist appears partially obscured by flora. Powerfully connecting the artist to nature and history, the work enacts a dynamic dialogue about identity alongside ideas of visibility and invisibility – of being seen and unseen. Through his declarative self portraiture, Thompson takes control of how First Nations people are represented, dismantling the legacies of ethnographic photography. \n\nThis is the first work that Thompson made following the failed Voice referendum of October 2023. Rather than his usual vibrant living blooms, it is an abundance of dried botanical specimens that envelop the artist. The photograph is sombre and reflective, its stillness interrupted only by the billowing ribbons. Referencing the hot desert air that Thompson fondly remembers from his childhood, the ribbons here symbolise hope in moving forward.

labels.title: Hibiscus rosa sinensis
labels.subtitle: Angela Tiatia
labels.medium: single channel digital video, colour, silent. 1 minute 30 seconds.

text.content:
single channel digital video, colour, silent. 1 minute 30 seconds.

Courtesy of Angela Tiatia and Sullivan+Strumpf

© Angela Tiatia. Courtesy of Sullivan+Strumpf.

Deeply embedded in her Sāmoan heritage, much of Angela Tiatia’s work critiques the commodification of Pacific Island women in Western popular culture. In _Hibiscus rosa sinensis_ the artist appears surrounded by lush foliage in close up, a bright red hibiscus protruding from her mouth. In traditional Sāmoan culture the hibiscus, when placed behind the ear, can signify relationship status and sexual availability. Behind the right means you’re looking; behind the left means you’re taken. In this video work, however, Tiatia creates a counternarrative. Challenging the stereotype of the sexually alluring ‘dusky maiden’, Tiatia stares steadily at the viewer and literally eats the myth. Tiatia’s consumption of this symbolically loaded bloom is an exercise in taking power back – she situates herself as protagonist and rejects the colonial gaze.

labels.title: Harirū
labels.subtitle: Sarah Hudson
labels.medium: single channel, digital video, colour, sound, 1 minute 50 seconds.

text.content: single channel, digital video, colour, sound, 1 minute 50 seconds.\n\n© Sarah Hudson

On a trip to Singapore, Aotearoa/New Zealand-based artist and researcher Sarah Hudson attempted to connect with intangible ancestors by gently ‘shaking hands’ with the native flowers and plants she encountered there. _Harirū_, which roughly translated from Te reo Māori means ‘hello’ or ‘cheers’, is a humorous response to the results of an online DNA test conducted by the artist that revealed her genetic makeup as ‘broadly Oceanic’ and ‘Southeast Asian’. In this video work, these simple and repetitive acts of attempted engagement lightheartedly address more nuanced themes of migration and identity.
gelatin silver photograph on paper

‘Tom invited me up to Wyndham, Western Australia to photograph him and his grandad, Steve just after Christmas. This was taken in between massive afternoon downpours. The roll of Italian, medium-format cinema film jammed in my camera. I managed to salvage it and store it in a light-tight pouch away from the brutal sun and the humidity of the wet season. Two days later that pouch was opened by a curious member of the Darwin Airport security team. A week later after processing it in Sydney, it appeared that the harsh Top End had left its mark on the film.’\n\nT W Baker is a documentary photographer based on Dharawal Country/Helensburgh, New South Wales.
type C photograph on paper

‘A contagious smile to shine through these challenging times. Meet Gail Mabo, a brilliant Meriam artist from Mer Island, Torres Strait and daughter of the legendary Dr Edward Koiki Mabo. This portrait was taken during an unplanned, spontaneous session following breakfast. As Gail shared cultural and personal stories, her vibrant personality and playful energy as a natural storyteller came to life, reflected in each frame captured. A former dancer and choreographer, Gail shared her dreams of returning to the theatre. I believe this photo perfectly captures her charisma and unstoppable spirit.’ \n\nEnzo Frisini is a Naarm/Melbourne-based photographer.
gelatin silver photograph on paper

‘This work is a portrait of my partner, Brandon Mathis, inside our Los Angeles studio. I printed the work myself in the darkroom. Brandon’s gaze, partially hidden by the fabric, along with the reaching hand and the rectangle of light that cuts right through him, creates a portrait that explores vulnerability, partnership dynamics and introspection. The fabric barrier becomes a metaphor for the complexities of relationships, knowing and unknowing, transparency and mystery. I also hope the viewer reflects on the delicate balance between exposure and privacy.’\n\nLos Angeles-based Australian photographer Luke Austin creates intimate portraits of members of the LGBTQIA+ community that reveal his subject’s individuality in a moment of disarming contact.
pigment photographic print on paper

‘The pregnant belly, a symbol of new life and transformation, is here framed between structures that represent both protection and confinement. I wanted the work to speak to the universal experience of transition, the tension between security and uncertainty, and the beauty found in the in-between spaces of life’s unfolding journey.’\n\nJennie Groom is a Tarntanya/Adelaide-based photographer whose practice focuses on portraiture, feminism and social commentary.
pigment photographic print on paper

‘Mahmoud looks across the courtyard at the Flemington Housing Estate towards the building where his parents live. In a few weeks, the already mostly vacated building would be torn down to make way for new flats. On our walk across the estate, we reflected on this new reality and what it means for his family and the many other families that once called this place home.’\n\nBasil Abubaker is a photographer based in Naarm/Melbourne whose work explores African-Australian identity through the perspectives of young African-Australians and their communities.
pigment photographic print on paper

‘The model Nakia is a proud Yuin and Gamilaroi woman, and also my partner. We planned for this shoot for a while to ensure everything was perfect. In tribute to Nakia’s Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander heritage, she wears a native floral headpiece I made for the photoshoot and a raffia skirt made by her mother. Nakia and I are both proud of our Indigenous heritage and often find it difficult to find balance between our culture and the modern world. We are both healthworkers trying to make a difference for our people.’\n\nSean Kinchela is a Gamilaroi and Wiradjuri man based on Yuin Country/Bodalla, New South Wales. His photography is influenced by his culture and the stories that come from the oceans, mountains, skies and land.
type C photograph on paper

‘Chhattisgarh is a state in central India that provides significant amounts of power to the country’s electricity grid. It is also the home of my closest friend in India and a place in which I photograph often. For the past three years I’ve dragged my friend out for early morning photo walks. On this day we met Pawan at a local pond where he had gone to bathe. He also showed us his pet turtle collection. The effects of Chhattisgarh’s power success story are writ large, both upon the rapidly urbanising landscape and within its residents’ collective awareness.’\n\nYask Desai is a Melbourne/Naarm-based visual artist of mixed Australian and Indian heritage. He works with photography, video, archives and text to explore themes of place and collective and individual identity, as well as critiquing global political economies.
pigment photographic print on paper

‘According to Reiki philosophy, hands are the silent messengers of the soul. The loving touch of a mother is something many of us know from childhood – it soothes, makes us feel safe, even heals a sore spot. A mother’s caress is a miraculous remedy because touch is the first true contact with life when entering the world. The essence of touch is deep, intimate and also magical. The photograph _Hands_ captures Ojas as he holds his parents’ hands while gently resting his hand on his mother’s growing belly.’\n\nLaura Zviedre is a Boorloo/Perth-based photographer whose documentary-style portraits capture honest and heartfelt stories.
pigment photographic print on paper

****

‘Every year, I ask my sons to direct a photographic portrait to give to each other as a gift. Johnny decided we must visit what he imagines to be his brother Richie’s future house for a pic. Upon arrival, I was met with a beautiful slice of Australian life in all its eccentricities. This pic captures sweet nostalgia, good intentions and the overall enduring heat when wanting to wear a Christmas sweater in Australia.’

Mrs White aka ABBIE DAVIS is a Naarm/Melbourne-based photographer whose colour-saturated works layer pop culture and nostalgia.
pigment photographic print on paper

‘_Code Black/Riot_ is a collaboration with a group of young First Nations people in far north Queensland that interrogates a system that targets and imprisons them from the age of 10. This portrait is from the still-photo component of the larger project, made together with Vernon Ah Kee and Behrouz Boochani. Participants were invited to have their portraits taken, using a means of their own choosing to conceal their identities while making a personal statement. Some of them chose flowers or bubbles. Others a flag, mask or face paint. The three girls here chose this gesture. Many young people in this community prefer to conceal their faces in their photos to avoid being identified by the youth justice system. The result is that many families do not have photos of their children if they lose them. The project was facilitated by Youth Empowered Towards Independence in Cairns and Change the Record in Sydney.’\n\nHoda Afshar is a Naarm/Melbourne-based visual artist and documentary maker whose practice focuses on the intricate relationships between politics and aesthetics, knowledge and representation, visibility and violence.
type C photograph on paper

‘Shorty lives in one of the most rural areas of New South Wales surrounded by national parks. This is his last year before going to boarding school where he hopes to follow his love of football and meet new friends. The portrait forms part of a long-term photographic project that focuses on documenting regional agricultural life in NSW and the transition between generations of farmers.’\n\nLuke David Kellett lives and works in Newcastle, NSW, on the Country of the Awabakal and Worimi peoples. His documentary photographs capture different parts of Australian life.

labels.title: Anna (Home for a Time)
labels.subtitle: (Nur) Aishah Kenton and Anna Jourdant
labels.medium: inkjet photographic print, mixed media and textiles

text.content: inkjet photographic print, mixed media and textiles

‘Sharehouse living has long been a rite of passage for young Australians. Once considered an important social conduit between adolescence and adulthood, it is now a more permanent housing solution for many Australians. Anna lives in a sharehouse in Meanjin/Brisbane, Queensland. “You learn a lot about yourself and what you value living in a sharehouse,” Anna says. Anna’s creative spirit inspired this mixed-media, collaborative portrait and she added the hand-colouring and embroidery. I knew any representation of her would need to be more than just a simple photo.’\n\n(Nur) Aishah Kenton is a Singapore-born, Malay artist based in Naarm/Melbourne who uses photography, installation and collaborative methods to weave together personal histories and broader narratives. For this portrait they have collaborated with Anna Jourdant, a Meanjin/Brisbane-based artist who works with stained glass, textiles and found objects.
pigment photographic print on paper

‘Sonny Jane is a queer, non-binary, disabled and neurodivergent advocate and writer, and defier of systems. “Since I was young, I was in and out of the mental health system where I was labelled as defiant, naughty and stubborn. My ‘defiant’ tattoo is a commitment to resist systems that pathologise our minds, bodies and queerness. My rainbow freckle tattoos are about wearing my personality and queerness on my face. When people look at me, they’re going to see who I am.” My work is often playful, but also aims to challenge in its own unexpected ways.’\n\nBri Hammond is an artist living and working on Kaurna land in Tarntanya/Adelaide. Hammond creates work that blends authentic emotion with wry observation to highlight the idiosyncrasies of people and places.
pigment photographic print on paper

‘This photograph captures a moment of quiet reflection as my mother rests on the couch with her cigarette. After six years in Australia away from Lebanon, I returned home to surprise her. She immediately prepared my favourite meals. Exhausted from cooking all day, she came to the salon, lit her cigarette, and lay down while pots simmered on the stove. My leaving home has strengthened our relationship in ways we never experienced when we lived under the same roof. The photo is a testament to our newfound appreciation and connection.’\n\nAyman Kaake is a Lebanon-born Australian photo-media artist based in Naarm/Melbourne. Through contemplative portraiture Kaake explores diasporic melancholy and the agony of exile.
pigment photographic print on paper

‘This work explores themes of renewal, identity and belonging. Wrapped in a golden emergency blanket and resting on a tumbled stone, my mother represents strength and adaptability, finding hope through her migration story. The golden blanket, a symbol of safety and care, contrasts with the grounding presence of the stone. Together, they reflect the tension between aspiration, humility, fragility and resilience. My practice explores the in-betweenness of identity, honouring my mother’s experience while encouraging viewers to reflect on their own connections to migration, home and the longing for belonging.’\n\nSherry Quiambao is an Australian-Filipino multidisciplinary artist based in Boorloo/Perth, Western Australia. Quiambao’s practice draws from personal and cultural narratives to question material culture and its role in shaping belonging and self-image.
pigment photographic print on paper

‘I am drawn in by the powers and potentials for in/visibility in portraiture, for playful evasion, for the performance of dis/appearing acts in which I pass before the camera un/seen. My interest in portraiture lies in the way artists – and I think queer artists in particular are quite attuned to this – can pervert the conditions of the portrait by toying with or complicating their own visibility. I am drawn toward portraiture that redistributes power toward the subject in interesting ways, allowing the subject to be the one who determines the ways, as well as the extent to which, they are seen by both camera and viewer.’\n\nGerwyn Davies is a queer artist based on Gadigal land/Sydney who works across photography, textile and costume. Davies takes a performative approach to photographic portraiture, exploring self-representation, camp aesthetics and kitsch Australiana.
type C photograph on paper

‘Thom Roberts at home in what he calls “Donkey Big Bird Station”, also known as Pennant Hills. I work with the artist Thom Roberts at Studio A and wanted to photograph him in his home environment. Thom sees and identifies his world and community through the lens of popular culture and the constructed world. For Thom, humans can be skyscrapers, the city’s infrastructure are his friends, and he is a CountryLink Express train. His perspective challenges the everyday humdrum of day-to-day life in this crazy world.’\n\nTanja Bruckner is a photographer based on Gadigal land/Sydney whose imagery aims to disrupt homogenous narratives and encourage inclusivity. Bruckner is driven by a strong focus on social justice and women’s and LGBTQIA+ rights. Studio A is a supported studio that creates professional pathways for artists with lived experience of intellectual disability.
type C photograph on paper

‘Oaklan’s story is extraordinary. Diagnosed with a rare disease affecting fewer than 500 boys worldwide, he’s faced more in his young life than most could imagine. At four, he underwent a bone marrow transplant, losing his hair to chemo – but then his mighty mullet was born. His journey includes countless life-saving surgeries, including a full spinal fusion last year, where his surgeon ensured his iconic hairstyle stayed intact. Though his school recently made him cut the mullet, his spirit remains unshaken. Oaklan is a walking miracle and a true Aussie legend.’ \n\nKristian Taylor-Wood is a London-born photographer, director and writer based on Gadigal land/Sydney whose portraits focus on unconventional and thought-provoking storytelling.
pigment photographic print on paper

‘Amazine and her father, Amaziah Club, took part in a men’s ceremony in Ampilatwatja, on the Alyawarre homelands, 325km northeast of Alice Springs. This image is from the series _Tea and Sugar_, which documents families involved in the landmark Stolen Wages class action against the Commonwealth Government in 2024. The lawsuit sought justice for the thousands of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples who worked on Northern Territory cattle stations between 1933 and 1971, receiving little or no wages – only “tea and sugar”. Amazine and Amaziah are descendants of Banjo Morton, who led the original protest for fair wages and recognition.’\n\nPaul Blackmore is a photojournalist based in Cavanbah/Byron Bay, New South Wales who covers social and political issues in Australia and internationally. His works explore the relationships between humanity and the extraordinary forces that shape our lives.

labels.title: You cannot leave it behind, for it will follow you
labels.subtitle: Georgia Wilde

text.content: pigment photographic print on paper

‘When I was younger, I used to feel that travel provided a temporary escape from life. I took this self portrait when I spent a week in tropical Ubud, Bali. It captures how it felt to try, and fail, at outrunning problems from home. It is unnatural to force the body to relax, and to let that discomfort be seen.’\n\nBased on Gadigal land/Sydney, Georgia Wilde works in video art, installation and mixed media. Her practice explores personal mythology, youth, womanhood and the body, and considers the way these themes interact with broader political and cultural landscapes.
pigment photographic print on paper

‘I made this photograph with Gamilaraay musician Thelma Plum on Wilyakali Country last winter. The image was shot using a Rolleicord camera on the Mundi Mundi Plains near the outback town of Broken Hill, where I live. We spent three days photographing throughout the far west New South Wales region, making a series of photographs for Thelma’s album _I’m Sorry, Now Say It Back_.’\n\nEm Jensen lives and works on Wilyakali Country in Broken Hill, NSW. Her practice explores themes of nostalgia, fringe subcultures and the Australian character, with a focus on sharing the stories of people in regional and remote parts of the country.
pigment photographic print on paper

‘As part of my series _Deep Heat_, I took this portrait “with” Antony Sinni, a non-binary friend/artist/icon (they/them) on Larrakia Land, Garramilla/Darwin. I highlight “with” because making an image like this is both individual and deeply collaborative. Entangled as a singular organism, experiencing what it is to be within a moment of vulnerability, connected by the sand, sky and the lens. This image sums up the series well, using photography to explore my complex relationship with gender and masculinity. Creating an opportunity for healing and evolution.’\n\nBased in Rubibi/Broome, Western Australia, multidisciplinary artist Matt Sav works across photography and film to investigate the intersection of gender, ecology, intimacy and isolation.
pigment photographic print on paper

‘Black Swan Theory originates from Europeans believing only white swans existed until they “visited” Western Australia in 1697, shocked to discover something so rare and beautiful as a Black Swan. The denial of the Maali/Black Swan serves as a greater metaphor for the failure of Europeans to acknowledge the beauty and sovereignty of First Nations peoples and their connection to this land, and the denial of queer people throughout history. This portrait is a tribute to WA, where Will grew up, and honours Garrett’s First Nations heritage, two queer swans floating in the romantic haze of a disco-enthused lake.’\n\nWill Huxley and Garrett Huxley are Naarm/Melbourne-based collaborative performance and visual artists. The Huxleys’ performances traverse the classifications of costume, film and recording in what they describe as a visual assault of sparkle, surrealism and the absurd.
pigment photographic print on paper

‘This image of Philomela is from _Lest We Forget_, a series of large photographic light paintings that recreate scenes from Greco-Roman mythology that depict rape. The work focuses on re-codifying mythological women’s experiences of rape to centre the victim-survivor. Through feminist intervention the work investigates the origins of the stories, alters perspective and challenges established gendered tropes.’\n\nDida Sundet is an interdisciplinary artist from Norway, based in Boorloo/Perth, who works with the photographic technique that she terms light painting, using long exposures to ‘paint’ with light. Her practice focuses on the connections between ancient mythology, traditional gender stereotypes and current sociopolitical debates around men’s violence towards women.
pigment print on Hahnemühle cotton rag satin paper

‘Ghyslaine Gau appears here as both subject and fragment. Born in Marseille to Martinican parents and raised in the suburbs of Paris, she is a dancer, performer and part of the collective Wusinsans with Constanza Mendoza and I. Inspired by Édouard Glissant’s concept of the abyss, this portrait – shot on film – gestures toward the unknown between self and other. It reflects the impossibility of fully capturing or knowing another, embracing relationality and absence. Resisting conventional portraiture, it offers a glimpse into a moment that remains incomplete, echoing the limits of language and perception.’\n\nShoufay Derz is an interdisciplinary artist working on Gadigal land/Sydney. Drawing on poetic and philosophical frameworks, her practice explores themes of transformation, absence and shared uncertainties.
pigment photographic print on paper

‘It’s a common feeling among the queer community – the deep desire to run into the forest, strip naked and just be “flaming faggots”. To reclaim the words that they call us and start our own rituals. For _FAGGOT MAGIC BONFIRE_, we wanted to embody this feeling. Informed by our shared queer experiences and an agreement to “make it super witchy”, we stoked the fire and created all our effects in-camera. Happy accidents and unexpected shapes form the final image, calling upon ancient queer magics that emerged from primordial body ooze, still sparkling and glistening long after we return to gay little dust.’\n\nJeff Andersen Jnr. is a Naarm/Melbourne-based photographer and filmmaker. His photographs explore movement, shape and shadow with themes of queer joy, amplifying mood through lighting and cinematic haze.
type C photograph & photogram on paper

‘When I visited Aida while she was living in Manila, Philippines, I made a portrait using a box camera built by local photographers Krystine Antonio, Jesed Moreno and Marion. Aida and I decorated the paper negative with her stickers, and then I rephotographed it. In November, Aida moved to Tarntanya/Adelaide to be with family, especially her sister who was about to have a baby. During this time I reshot the print, adding a Baby Taz sticker Aida sent me years ago, and a jelly centipede on the paper as a photogram.’\n\nTarntanya/Adelaide-based artist Emmaline Zanelli creates constructed compositions that amplify the expressive power of objects and images. Her portraits explore the relationship between photography, memory and identity.
pigment photographic print on paper

‘17-year resident John Patmore is one of 32 battlers living in four old boarding houses in Sydney’s Paddington. “I thought I’d be able to live here ’til the day I die after my super started to run out. But now … This place is going to close on January 31, and we’re all being kicked out. I’ve got nowhere to go. It’s just so overwhelming.” It is a classic battle between a developer who wants to push residents out for profit and a vulnerable community who have called this boarding house home for many years. Where do they go now?’ \n\nDion Georgopoulos is a multimedia journalist and filmmaker based on Gadigal land/Sydney who shares his passion for visual storytelling across photography and video.
pigment photographic print on paper

‘Dame Stan Munro, aged 84, is arguably Australia’s oldest performing drag queen. Born in Wales, he made his debut as a child actor/performer before touring the United Kingdom, Ireland and Germany. In 1963, Stan migrated to Australia as a “Ten Pound Pom” where he discovered drag after joining the Sydney review show, _Les Girls_. He became their star performer and compere and in the 1970s continued with Melbourne _Les Girls_. After meeting him at the local gym in Kyogle, New South Wales, where he lives, I was compelled to photograph him. His portrait forms part of a growing body of portraits in rural Kyogle.’\n\nJodie Harris is a Yamatji and Wadjari photographer working on Bundjalung Country in northern New South Wales. Her experimental practice crosses the genres of environmental portraiture and social documentary.
pigment photographic print on paper

‘This photo captures the intimate chaos of a life lived in one space, my childhood bedroom, for 34 years. Surrounded by a tapestry of trinkets and the quiet clutter of everyday life, my self portrait reflects the intersection of nostalgia and the present. The disorder becomes a tangible reminder of time passing, of a personal history that clings to the space, the room itself as much a part of my identity as the person within it.’\n\nTom Zust is a Slovenian-Australian artist and photographer who lives and works on the land of the Darug people in Western Sydney. Zust is engaged in the relationship between identity, artifice and photographic depiction.
pigment photographic print on paper

‘_Lurking_ is a self portrait created at the Leveld Kunstnartun art residency in rural Norway. The photograph features custom-made leather harnesses I made during the residency. Set against the rural backdrop, the urban leather contrasts with the weathered wood, symbolising the tension between isolation and resilience often present in queer experiences. The work invites viewers to contemplate queerness as both an inner journey and an ongoing dialogue with the world around us.’\n\nDerek Sargent is a Tarntanya/Adelaide-based artist whose practice includes moving image, sculpture, installation and photography. His work investigates themes of sexual identity and authenticity and questions dominant histories and narratives.
type C photograph on paper

‘This is a portrait of Kyanne, who enjoys fishing, spending afternoons with family, and hopes to work with children in the future. The photograph was taken at the 2024 NAIDOC Ball, which fosters confidence, belief, courage and hope among the youth of Aurukun. Aurukun is home to five main spiritual clan groups: Apalech, Winchanam, Wanam, Chara and Puutch.’ \n\nDavid Prichard is a photographer based on Bundjalung Country/Byron Bay whose portraits create space for the subject to command the frame.
tintype, Chromaluxe metal print

‘Rav, depicted here, is a proud Indian trans man, born in Singapore, who moved to Australia for love and queer community. “It took me a long time to come to terms with my queer and trans identity while shutting down the voices in my head that say Indians can’t be queer or trans and that it’s a whitewashed thing. It’s innate for me. I was born this way and to be able to celebrate my identity along with my culture is my biggest achievement.”’\n\nPrue Hazelgrove is a Queer Canberra-based artist living on unceded Ngunnawal Country who specialises in the 19th-century wet plate collodion process. The historical significance of this medium enables them to represent stories erased from the narrative.
wet plate collodion tintype

‘Wet plate collodion was the pre-eminent photographic technology of the 1860s. The images exist as highlights of pure silver metal on a black background. The process is arcane, alchemical and potentially explosive. Fickle too, as a tiny deviation in the workflow may result in the complete absence of emulsion. Muni kindly sat for a portrait in my Pomona studio. We relinquished our control to the process, and it imposed its will upon us. In our flaws we find our greatest strength.’\n\nRaoul Slater is a photographer from Pomona on the land of the Kabi Kabi/Gubbi Gubbi peoples on Queensland’s Sunshine Coast. He has recently been working in the medium of wet plate collodion.
pigment photographic print on paper

‘This is a very special place for me and my family at the river Langi. Being on Country means reconnecting with nature and its healing powers. My Country and family are recurring subjects in my work. The child in the picture is Violet, my niece, who is about to throw the net. Children learn from the age of five how to handle a fishing net. The picture is a representation of belonging to Country and Violet personifies the pride that goes with belonging, skills, knowing about your heritage and living within your culture.’\n\nNyikina, Yawuru, Ngul Ngul and Ngarinyin photographer Mary-Lou Orliyarli Divilli was born in Derby, Western Australia. She is inspired by older generations and their depth of cultural knowledge expressed through stories and traditional song.
gelatin silver photograph on paper

‘Intimacy can be so much more than the popular definition. This photo depicts Kayleb painting up his partner Britney in a design that speaks of love and loss. Britney and Kayleb are both incredible cultural dancers and would later that evening dance together to further display the depth of love and loss that they have experienced. Culture is a powerful healer. Waagan Galga Corroboree, Wonnarua Country, November 2024.’ \n\nBiripi and Dhungutti photographer Marcus Rowsell lives on Worimi Country in Forster, New South Wales. He shoots primarily in black-and-white 35mm film, a deliberate choice to strip away complexity and focus on the raw beauty of everyday moments.
pigment photographic print on paper

‘Zev and Nick met 15 years ago and have been together for eight years. Their relationship developed over time and they now share a home and often a bed. They have plans for the future. Zev is queer and seven months into their relationship began medically transitioning. Nick also has a girlfriend and they have been going out for three years. Zev was not in any other relationship at the time of the portrait. Zev and Nick’s relationship is non-monogamous and they love each other.’\n\nHilary Wardhaugh is a photographer, artist and activist based on Ngunnawal and Ngambri land in Canberra whose practice explores humanity and the environment through a feminist lens.
pigment photographic print on paper

‘This photograph was made in the home of Sarah and Dylan, five months after the sudden passing of their late husband and father, Norman. Norman was my GP who helped me with lifesaving medication through suicidal depression. Following his death I temporarily moved into Sarah and Dylan’s apartment to help support them. I created this image using a bedside lamp held in my hand and a subtle front swing. Sarah, an ex-sex worker and a lifelong photographic artist, has been a champion of mine since the beginning of my photographic journey back in 2015.’\n\nRyan Dandelion is a portrait artist based on Bundjalung land/Lismore working with analogue photography and printing techniques. Dandelion’s practice explores themes of vulnerability, sexuality, and the relationship between photographer and subject.
pigment photographic print on paper

‘“Queer people must literally construct the houses they will be born into” – Nayland Blake. I return to ideas about home and queerness regularly in my work. Home is a place I’ve run from most of my life; home is a place I’ve needed to find. On a recent trip interstate, I visited my friend Riley and his partner James. Seeing them so at ease with each other in their home reminded me of the peace I’ve found at home with my partner. In this shallow focus close-up, Riley frames James in an over-the-shoulder embrace.’\n\nTorrey Bruinstroop is a photographer and writer who lives in Magandjin/Brisbane. Bruinstroop combines analogue in-camera techniques with minimal digital editing to create images that are rooted in the aesthetics of 20th-century queer photographers such as David Wojnarowicz, George Platt Lynes and Peter Hujar.

labels.title: Grace Tame – Between the Sea and the Sky
labels.subtitle: Stuart Spence

text.content: pigment photographic print on paper

‘People are malleable entities, adapting, skewing, feeling, often hundreds of times a day. So when an artist creates a portrait, a question needs to be asked, who exactly is being captured? The idea of a single artwork somehow portraying a sitter’s character or persona seems fraught to me. Grace Tame is extraordinary, living a life few could comprehend, let alone survive, devoting her life these days to advocating for survivors of child sexual abuse. What story did she bring to my portrait this day? What part of her spirit did she choose to reveal? Is this even a portrait of Grace Tame? Maybe, but of course there’s so much more, a ripple on an ocean that holds beneath far more than it reveals. Oh, but what a ripple.’\n\nStuart Spence is a photographer and director based on Gadigal land/Sydney whose practice spans portraiture, reportage, film and fine art.
pigment photographic print on paper

‘_Persona_ places my face amidst a cityscape also featuring my friend, artistic collaborator Joey Gala. In this portrait, my face is reflected in Gala’s “mask”, noting our shared Aboriginality and place. We were both raised in Badtjala Country; I was adopted into a white family (with subsequent confusion about my identity), and Gala raised in community (grounded in culture). I ponder our lives – and potential parallels – in this personal exploration that probes the materialistic conditioning and emptiness of Western society (which exists in contrast to the connectivity Captain Cook observed in Aboriginal traditional lives back in 1770).’\n\nMichael Cook is a Meanjin/Brisbane-based contemporary photographic artist of Bidjara heritage. Cook’s works interrogate the legacy of colonisation and invite the viewer to experience roles in reversal and histories re-written.
type C photograph on paper

‘I took this self portrait on 35mm film using Portra 400. I wanted to capture the fleeting moment of freedom felt within when untethered from responsibility. A chance to live life on my terms. The photograph depicts this feeling of longing, joy and excitement. It is natural, candid and captures the true feeling of the moment.’\n\nTiarni Majid-Loban is a proud Wuthathi woman with connections to the Torres Strait who lives and works on Yuwi Country/Mackay, Queensland. Her photography documents the present for the future.
pigment photographic print on paper

‘In the evenings when I drove up a small laneway off Norton Street, Leichhardt I used to often see Antonio Intili at work through his shop window. Born in Capizzi, Sicily in 1935 and a tailor in Leichhardt for close to 60 years, he appeared an elfin figure sitting on his work benchtop late into the evening cutting, stitching and pressing anything from jeans to the finest Italian fabrics. Until recently, he was truly one of the very last of the original Italian immigrants to still be working, and a proud ambassador of the once thriving Little Italy that Leichhardt was famous for ...’\n\nGeorge Fetting is a visual artist and photographer based on Gadigal land/Sydney who specialises in portrait, travel and editorial photography. He describes portraiture as his ‘first love’. The subject of this photograph, Antonio Intili, sadly passed away in July 2025.
lambda photographic print on paper

‘This portrait of Annabelle and Dakota captures a crucial moment in their self-discovery – two tweens standing at the edge of adolescence, exploring and questioning their identities. It reflects the fragility and strength of their transformation as they navigate a world that sometimes demands they grow up too quickly. Through their expressions and presence, the photograph meditates on the delicate balance between innocence and maturity, as well as the quiet intensity of young people searching for their place in an ever-evolving world. It’s a visual exploration of the complexities of identity during a time of rapid change.’\n\nNatalie Grono is a photo artist and filmmaker based in Nyangbul/Lennox Head, New South Wales. With a foundation in journalism, Grono is a passionate storyteller whose engaging style reveals the extraordinary in the everyday.
pigment photographic print on paper

‘Kalkadunga man William Barton is renowned worldwide as a yidaki (didgeridoo) player, composer, producer, multi-instrumentalist and vocalist. He has worked with orchestras, string quartets and classical ensembles internationally, infusing classical traditions with his musicality and Kalkadunga culture.’\n\nNeil Bennett is an artist based on Gadigal land/Sydney who has worked in visual storytelling for over three decades and has a passion for portrait photography.
pigment photographic print on paper

‘Kodjdjan, Mary Kolkkiwara Nadjamerrek, aged 93, of the Wakmarranj clan, is one of the last surviving speakers of the critically endangered Dalabon language. Mary holds a Waddan (frill neck lizard) that her grandchildren caught while camping on her father’s Country, Momob, in Central Arnhem Land.’ \n\nRenae Saxby is a documentary photographer based on Wonnarua Country/Maitland, New South Wales. Her work is an exploration of the human experience, from intimate portraiture to remote landscapes with a focus on cultural and environmental preservation.
pigment photographic print on paper

‘Megan is my friend and neighbour. Her life blends the ordinary with the extraordinary. Against the backdrop of her traditional Australian weatherboard house, Megan stoically stands with one of her rescue camels while her daughter energetically bounces on the trampoline. I wanted to capture this moment and preserve the elements of both domesticity and adventure which encapsulate her life.’\n\nRebecca Polonski lives and works on Taungurung Country in regional Victoria. Through her photography, she seeks to capture surreal moments within the everyday.
pigment photographic print on paper

‘This portrait features Tiwi artist Alfonso Puautjimi, a member of the Ngaruwanajirri Art Centre in Wurrumiyanga, Tiwi Islands. Alfonso has been painting with Ngaruwanajirri, which supports artists with lived experience of disability and fosters community and collaboration, since 1997. The art centre’s name means “helping one another”. The bicycle, featured in both his art and this portrait, serves as a personal inspiration to him. In 2024, one of his bicycle paintings was selected as a finalist in the NATSIAA (National Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Art Awards). Captured with a large-format camera using Polaroid Type 665 film, this portrait highlights Alfonso’s quiet strength and enduring spirit.’\n\nNaarm/Melbourne-based Tobias Titz is a photographer and educator who has worked with First Nations communities in the Pilbara, Kimberley, Western Desert, Arnhem Land, Tiwi Islands and Central Australia for over 15 years.

labels.title: Les Darcy And Jeff Smith Putting Their Gloves On
labels.subtitle: Unknown artist

text.content: gelatin silver photograph on paper\n\nNational Film and Sound Archive. Donated by William Carty, 1994

By the time Les Darcy was 18, Sydney boxing promoters had begun to import top international opponents to challenge him, including American middleweight champion Jerome ‘Jeff Smith’ Jefferds (1891–1962). Although the pair only met twice in Australia, their rivalry became one of the defining chapters in both fighters’ careers. In their first fight in January 1915, Smith won by default after Darcy withdrew following a low blow. In their September 1916 rematch, Smith was disqualified for a groin punch, giving Darcy the win. A rumoured third fight in New Orleans in April 1917 never eventuated and Darcy died soon after.
oil on linen\n\nMaitland Regional Art Gallery. Purchased 2021

Les Darcy (1895–1917) had a short but brilliant boxing career. He earned his first pay check as a boxer at age 14, and as a professional won 52 out of 56 fights against some of the best middleweight champions in the world. Darcy’s skill, strength, boyish looks and working-class background quickly made him a national hero. He notoriously fled to New York in 1916, after his mother refused to consent for him to enlist in the First World War as he was under 21. Following the controversy, American promoters lost interest in him, and six months later he was admitted to hospital in Memphis where he died of septicaemia at age 21. About 250,000 people are said to have lined Sydney’s streets as Darcy’s coffin made its way back toward his hometown Maitland. In Nigel Milsom’s portrait, the monochromatic figure of Darcy materialises as a ghost-like apparition. Set against the inky black void of the background, the fractured outline of Darcy’s body emphasises his deft agility; he is lithe and ready to attack. In the interplay between light and dark, action and stillness, strength and fragility, Milsom muses on the mythos of the working-class hero.
collage print on paper\n\nMaitland Regional Art Gallery. Purchased 2020

Wiradjuri artist Karla Dickens’ _Hokey Pokey_ and _Pound 4 Pound_ derive from a larger body of work that explores the complex history of First Nations circus and boxing tent performers. From the late 19th century to the 1960s, troupes of talented boxers toured regional Australia, taking on local challengers. Many young men, particularly First Nations men, joined travelling boxing tents as a way to earn a living and, for some, as a pathway to professional boxing. The title _Pound 4 Pound_ references the Jimmy Sharman’s Troupe slogan ‘A round or two for a pound or two’, used to attract young men to join the brutal life on the road.\n\nCollage and assemblage are essential aspects of Dickens’ practice. In these prints, the artist brings together historical and contemporary imagery to comment on the exploitation experienced by First Nations tent-boxers and circus performers, and the racism and injustice that continues in Australia today. Describing this series, Dickens wrote: ‘These Aboriginal faces reflect historical human zoos, sadly mirrored today in jails countrywide … Mr Ready firmly stands his ground, not just a boxing-tent performer, his fists help him to protect and survive – day in, day out.’
collage print on paper\n\nMaitland Regional Art Gallery. Purchased 2020

Wiradjuri artist Karla Dickens’ _Hokey Pokey_ and _Pound 4 Pound_ derive from a larger body of work that explores the complex history of First Nations circus and boxing tent performers. From the late 19th century to the 1960s, troupes of talented boxers toured regional Australia, taking on local challengers. Many young men, particularly First Nations men, joined travelling boxing tents as a way to earn a living and, for some, as a pathway to professional boxing. The title _Pound 4 Pound_ references the Jimmy Sharman’s Troupe slogan ‘A round or two for a pound or two’, used to attract young men to join the brutal life on the road.\n\nCollage and assemblage are essential aspects of Dickens’ practice. In these prints, the artist brings together historical and contemporary imagery to comment on the exploitation experienced by First Nations tent-boxers and circus performers, and the racism and injustice that continues in Australia today. Describing this series, Dickens wrote: ‘These Aboriginal faces reflect historical human zoos, sadly mirrored today in jails countrywide … Mr Ready firmly stands his ground, not just a boxing-tent performer, his fists help him to protect and survive – day in, day out.’
mixed media\n\nMaitland Regional Art Gallery. Purchased 2020

Karla Dickens’ _Pound-for-Pound # 3_ is part of a series of sculptures that pay homage to the strength, pride and resilience of First Nations boxing-tent performers. Embellished with leather fringing and feathers, a cast aluminium boxing glove stands atop a fence post, a symbol of the longstanding fight for Aboriginal Land Rights and sovereignty. Made from found materials that carry the symbolic weight of the past, this haunting sculpture layers place, history and memory.

type C photograph on paper

Collection: National Portrait Gallery Gift of the artist 2004

© Estate of Penny Tweedie

In this photograph, Pertame boxer James Swan (b. 1974) teaches young First Nations people how to spar in his hometown of Mparntwe/Alice Springs, Northern Territory. Swan began boxing at nine years old to defend himself against bullies. A disciplined fighter, he quickly rose to fame in the featherweight and bantamweight boxing divisions, winning his first Australian title in 1993. He won bronze medals at the Commonwealth Games in 1994 and 1998, and represented Australia at the 1996 and 2000 Olympics.\n\nAs a young boxer, Swan was inspired by Gunditjmara boxer Lionel Rose MBE, recalling: ‘I was at a dinner one night at Kumantjayi Perkins’ house in Sydney when Lionel put his arm around me and said, “James, you’re gonna be the next world champion.” That meant a lot to me because Lionel is my hero. I’ve always tried to copy his style of fighting.’ This portrait, from Penny Tweedie’s larger series _Standing Strong_, shows Swan carrying on his mentor’s legacy.
digital photographic still on paper\n\nNational Film and Sound Archive. Courtesy Pursekey Productions

In 2010, photographer Hamish Cairns created a series of black-and-white stills for the documentary _Boxing for Palm Island_, directed by Adrian Russell-Wills. Located 65 kilometres northeast of Townsville in Queensland, Bwgcolman/Palm Island has a dark history of colonial oppression and discrimination. Cairns’ photographs are portraits of grit and resilience that spotlight the strength and spirit of the Bwgcolman people. Through his lens, Cairns captured the raw determination and pride of aspiring boxers who found purpose and direction within the walls of the local gym.

 

One of the most compelling figures is 26-year-old Bwgcolman woman Noby Clay. A featherweight boxer – just 45 kilograms – and a mother of three, Clay trains with a strength and precision that defies the stereotypes often placed on her community. Coached by the late Uncle Ray Dennis, she approaches boxing with the disciplined grace of a dancer, revealing both the power and the expressive potential of the sport.

For Clay, stepping into the ring is part of a long family legacy. ‘I think I’m in the right sport because my father was a world-ranked boxer, my grandfathers Freddy Clay and Alfie Clay were boxers, all my uncles boxed, my brothers boxed, my cousins boxed, one of my aunties boxed and all the women in my family are strong women.’

inkjet print

Collection: National Portrait Gallery Gift of the artist 2006

© Toni Wilkinson

Five-time world champion boxer Sharon Anyos (b. 1970) is the first Australian woman to win a world boxing title. Anyos started karate at age six, and by 16 was a national champion in both karate and kickboxing. She made her pro boxing debut in 1998 at Australia’s first-ever officially sanctioned women’s fight, winning the junior lightweight title. Trained by boxing legend Jeff Fenech, she won her first world featherweight title just two years later.\n\nToni Wilkinson’s photograph of Anyos was taken in 2006, the year she won her fifth world featherweight title. The work is from a larger group portrait of five women athletes portrayed outside of the context of their sport. Using tight-cropping and strong studio lighting, Wilkinson presents a raw and penetrating portrait of Anyos. It reflects on the power, passion, pain and punishment – both mental and physical – required to excel in sport. Anyos retired in 2007 with 14 wins (four by knockout) from 17 fights and was inducted into the International Women’s Boxing Hall of Fame in 2020.

gelatin silver photograph on paper

Collection: National Portrait Gallery Purchased 2003

© Michael McQuillan's Classic Photographs

Boxing trainer Ern McQuillan (1905–1988) coached more national boxing champions than any other trainer in Australia. This photograph was taken by his son, Ern McQuillan OAM, a former boxer turned press photographer. The legendary trainer is pictured in his Newtown gym leaning against the ropes of the ring.In the background, walls are lined with the artist’s photographs of his father’s champion boxers including Tony Mundine AO (Bundjalung), Vic Patrick, Jack Hassen (Kalkadoon) and Bobby Dunlop.\n\nA professional boxer before opening his first gym in 1928, McQuillan’s first Australian champion Pat Craig won the bantamweight title in 1933. For the following three decades, McQuillan dominated Australian boxing, training 38 national champions and six Commonwealth champions. McQuillan moved to his third and final Newtown premises in 1974, where he continued to train boxers until the week before his death. His family recall that ‘every day of his life he went to the gym, came home, had his tea and fell asleep’.

screenprint on five sheets of paper

Gift of Brook Andrew in memory of Emmaline Rose Charnock 2012. Donated through the Australian Government's Cultural Gifts Program.

© Brook Andrew/Copyright Agency, 2024

Bundjalung boxer Anthony Mundine (b. 1975) was a rugby league star, playing for the St George Dragons and the Brisbane Broncos, before turning to professional boxing in 2000. His subsequent tally of victories include the 2003 and 2007 super-middleweight world title and the 2009 middleweight world title. More than an athlete, Mundine is also a role model and a vocal spokesperson on issues such as racism in sport who, since converting to Islam in the 1990s, abstains from alcohol and tobacco. In this work, Wiradjuri artist Brook Andrew toys with the image of the hero in contemporary media culture. Constructed from collaged screenprints, the portrait features Wiradjuri visual and written language including motifs that derive from shields and dendroglyphs. The words in language that extend from Mundine’s outstretched arms are Ngajuu ngaay nginduugirr (I see you) and Nginduugirr ngaay ngajuu (You see me). Atop the text, Andrew emblazons two cigarette packets with the words ‘Hope’ and ‘Peace’, reconfiguring advertising tropes to challenge the way we consume meaning through media as well as inherited ideas of masculinity. Viewer and subject are bound together, each one seeing the other, all too aware of the way stereotyping can affect and alter perception.
ink on paper\n\nNational Film and Sound Archive

Boxing enjoyed great popularity in Australia in the early 20th century, particularly throughout the Great Depression in the 1930s. Serving as affordable entertainment and as a potential source of income for many, its widespread appeal led numerous film productions to capitalise on the sport’s prominence. This poster uses a dramatic silhouette design to entice audiences to come and see the ‘World’s greatest fight film’, _On With The Gloves_. The 1938 documentary drew on some of Australia’s earliest moving-image records and combined them with rare international footage to satisfy sports fans. Featured bouts included Les Darcy’s 1916 victory over American middleweight Jimmy Clabby; the 1922 Paris match in which Senegalese fighter Amadou Louis M’Barick Fall aka ‘Battling Siki’ stunned audiences by knocking out French champion Georges Carpentier to become the first African-born world boxing champion; and the 1910 contest between Giabal/Jarowair boxer Jerry Jerome and rugby union star and boxer Arthur Cripps at Brisbane’s Olympic Park.

three-channel video installation, black and white, sound, 5:00 mins 
Director: Tara Marynowsky 
Producer and editor: Rachael Priddel 
Production company: See How It Plays

National Film and Sound Archive Archival footage courtesy Seven Network

This poetic moving-image portrait repurposes footage of 19-year-old Gunditjmara man Lionel Rose MBE, captured just days before his victorious bantamweight world title fight against Masahiko ‘Fighting’ Harada\n\non 27 February 1968 at Nippon Budokan Stadium in Chiyoda, Tokyo. In the lead-up to the event, Rose trained with relentless dedication – running through the winter snow and committing to six rigorous rounds a day in the gym, skipping, sparring and boxing alongside his trainer Jack Rennie. Originally filmed for television’s _World of Sport_, the incomplete footage has been reimagined to illuminate Rose’s unwavering confidence and fighting spirit.

labels.title: Lionel Rose, World Champion Bantam Weight Boxer before departing to the USA to defend his title
labels.subtitle: Mervyn Bishop

text.content:
gelatin silver photograph on paper

Purchased 2008

© Mervyn Bishop

Gunditjmara world champion boxer Lionel Rose MBE (1948–2011) is the first First Nations person to win a world sporting title. Rose took up boxing in his early teens, having been introduced to the sport by his father, Roy, a tent-show fighter. The day after Roy’s death in 1963, Rose won the Australian flyweight championship, aged 15. The eldest of nine, he turned professional to help support his family, taking out the national bantamweight title in 1966. At 19, Rose went to Tokyo to contest the world bantamweight belt in a bout against Masahiko ‘Fighting’ Harada. Rose defeated Harada on points after a 15-round fight and returned to Melbourne a national hero – more than 250,000 people turned out to welcome him home. In this portrait, Murri photographer Mervyn Bishop captures Rose at the pinnacle of his success during a press conference at Sydney airport in 1968, prior to defending his world title in the USA. Rose’s experience in Japan is documented in the video work _Lionel Rose in Tokyo 1968_ (2019), displayed next to this photograph.
pigment print reproduction of cellulose acetate negative\n\nNational Portrait Gallery. Purchased 2015
Giabal/Jarowair boxer Jerry Jerome (1874–1943) is the first First Nations person to win a national boxing title, claiming the Australian middleweight championship in 1912.
gelatin silver photographs on paper\n\nNational Portrait Gallery. Purchased 2011

Clem (1917–1989), Percy (1921–1974), George (1924–1986), Dave (1926–1952), Alfie (1929–1985) and Russell Ritchie (1937–1977), better known as the ‘Fighting Sands Brothers’, were members of one of Australia’s most remarkable sporting families. Of Dunghutti/Thunggutti descent, the brothers claimed numerous victories in a period spanning 20 years, notching up 607 bouts between them and winning a third of that total by knockout.
gelatin silver photographs on paper\n\nNational Portrait Gallery. Purchased 2011

Clem (1917–1989), Percy (1921–1974), George (1924–1986), Dave (1926–1952), Alfie (1929–1985) and Russell Ritchie (1937–1977), better known as the ‘Fighting Sands Brothers’, were members of one of Australia’s most remarkable sporting families. Of Dunghutti/Thunggutti descent, the brothers claimed numerous victories in a period spanning 20 years, notching up 607 bouts between them and winning a third of that total by knockout.
